title: QUIC RFC 9000 STREAM_DATA_BLOCKED Runtime Emission Architecture
purpose: Describe the bounded active-runtime path that emits a protected `STREAM_DATA_BLOCKED` advisory frame when an application stream write is blocked by the peer's current stream-level flow-control limit.
design_summary: When an application write reaches the active runtime and `QuicConnectionStreamState.TryReserveSendCapacity` reports stream-level send credit exhaustion, the runtime preserves the failed-write boundary and attempts to emit a protected `STREAM_DATA_BLOCKED` application packet through the existing blocked-signal send helper. The advisory signal is emitted only when no ack-eliciting packet and no retransmission is already in flight, uses the blocked stream's current send limit as `Maximum Stream Data`, and is tracked by the existing application-data sender ledger for loss repair. A later real `MAX_STREAM_DATA` increase remains the only mechanism that makes the blocked write admissible.
data_and_state_considerations: The slice does not add public write queuing or a new flow-control scheduler. The write still fails fast with the existing unsupported waiting-credit error, the stream bookkeeping remains responsible for identifying stream-level exhaustion, and the sent advisory packet is retained by the normal sender/recovery ledger until acknowledgment or loss handling.
edge_cases_and_constraints: `STREAM_DATA_BLOCKED` emission must not commit stream data or fabricate write success.
The emitted frame must use stream scope rather than connection or stream-type scope.
The emitted Stream ID and Maximum Stream Data values must identify the blocked stream and the current stream send limit that caused blocking.
The runtime must not emit a duplicate advisory packet while ack-eliciting work or retransmission is already outstanding.
Loss of the protected advisory packet must keep the packet available to the sender/recovery retransmission path.
risks: Conflating connection-level and stream-level blocking would emit the wrong blocked-frame type.
Treating advisory emission as queued-write support would overstate the public stream contract.
Emitting repeatedly while packets are already in flight could add avoidable ack-eliciting churn.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers only active-runtime advisory `STREAM_DATA_BLOCKED` emission for stream-level flow-control exhaustion on application stream writes. It does not claim write queuing until later credit, receiver-side flow-control autotuning, broader scheduler behavior, `0-RTT`, key update, migration, or public API changes.
